title: A seat on the train to Hue
summary: A young engineer finds a seat on a long train. The retired teacher next to him guesses his job from his shoes.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: Excuse me. Is this seat free?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Yes. Please sit down.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: Thank you. I have been standing for an hour.

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: An hour? Where did you get on?

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: Vinh. I am going to Saigon for a meeting. You?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: I am going home. Hue. I have lived there for forty years.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: You have been to Saigon, then?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Many times. I used to teach there. Now I am retired.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: What did you teach?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Reading. I taught children how to read. For thirty years.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: That is a long time with one subject.

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Reading is never the same thing twice. A child who learns 'cat' is different from a child who learns 'enough'. The road from 'cat' to 'enough' takes seven years.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: Do you miss it?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Some days. Most days, no. I read for myself now.

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: What are you reading?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: A book about old bridges. Are you an engineer?

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: How did you know?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Your shoes. They are clean, but the soles are worn at strange places. Engineers walk over things, not on roads. Am I right?

body.speaker: Engineer
body.text: You are right.

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: I am still a teacher. I cannot help it.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect continuous: 'have been + V-ing'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành tiếp diễn (have/has been + V-ing) cho một hành động bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn đang tiếp diễn đến hiện tại. Trong bài: 'I have been standing for an hour.' (Tôi đã đứng được một tiếng đồng hồ.) So sánh với hiện tại hoàn thành đơn 'I have lived there for forty years.' — cũng bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn tiếp tục.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the present perfect continuous (have/has been + V-ing) for an action that started in the past and is still happening. From the dialogue: 'I have been standing for an hour.' Compare with the simple present perfect: 'I have lived there for forty years.'

cloze.template:
Engineer: Excuse me. Is this seat free?
Teacher: Yes. Please sit down.
Engineer: Thank you. I have been {{b1}} for an hour.
Teacher: An hour? Where did you get on?
Engineer: {{b2}}. I am going to {{b3}} for a meeting. You?
Teacher: I am going home. {{b4}}. I have lived there for {{b5}} years.
Engineer: You have been to Saigon, then?
Teacher: Many times. I used to teach there. Now I am {{b6}}.
Engineer: What did you teach?
Teacher: {{b7}}. I taught children how to read. For {{b8}} years.
Engineer: That is a long time with one subject.
Teacher: Reading is never the same thing twice. A child who learns 'cat' is different from a child who learns 'enough'. The road from 'cat' to 'enough' takes {{b9}} years.
Engineer: Do you miss it?
Teacher: Some days. Most days, no. I read for myself now.
Engineer: What are you reading?
Teacher: A book about old {{b10}}. Are you an {{b11}}?
Engineer: How did you know?
Teacher: Your {{b12}}. They are clean, but the soles are worn at strange places. Engineers walk over things, not on roads. Am I right?
Engineer: You are right.
Teacher: I am still a teacher. I cannot help it.

blanks.explanation: He has been standing for an hour — that is why he wants the seat.
The engineer got on at Vinh.
He is going to Saigon for a meeting.
The teacher is going home to Hue.
She has lived in Hue for forty years.
She used to teach; now she is retired.
She taught reading — for thirty years.
The road from 'cat' to 'enough' takes seven years — how a child grows as a reader.
She is reading a book about old bridges.
She guesses he is an engineer — from his shoes.
His shoes give him away — soles worn at strange places.

translationVi:
Kỹ sư: Xin lỗi. Ghế này còn trống không ạ?
Giáo viên: Còn. Mời cậu ngồi.
Kỹ sư: Cảm ơn. Cháu đã đứng được một tiếng rồi.
Giáo viên: Một tiếng cơ à? Cậu lên ở đâu?
Kỹ sư: Vinh. Cháu đang đi Sài Gòn để họp. Cô thì sao ạ?
Giáo viên: Cô đang đi về nhà. Huế. Cô đã sống ở đó bốn mươi năm.
Kỹ sư: Vậy cô đã đến Sài Gòn nhiều lần rồi ạ?
Giáo viên: Nhiều lần. Trước cô từng dạy ở đó. Bây giờ cô nghỉ hưu rồi.
Kỹ sư: Cô dạy môn gì ạ?
Giáo viên: Tập đọc. Cô dạy trẻ con đọc. Suốt ba mươi năm.
Kỹ sư: Một môn học mà ba mươi năm thì dài thật.
Giáo viên: Đọc không bao giờ là cùng một thứ hai lần. Một đứa trẻ học từ 'cat' khác một đứa trẻ học từ 'enough'. Con đường từ 'cat' đến 'enough' mất bảy năm.
Kỹ sư: Cô có nhớ nghề không ạ?
Giáo viên: Có những ngày có. Phần lớn các ngày thì không. Bây giờ cô đọc cho chính mình.
Kỹ sư: Cô đang đọc gì ạ?
Giáo viên: Một cuốn sách về những cây cầu cũ. Cậu là kỹ sư phải không?
Kỹ sư: Sao cô biết ạ?
Giáo viên: Đôi giày của cậu. Sạch, nhưng đế mòn ở những chỗ kỳ lạ. Kỹ sư đi trên các vật, không phải trên đường. Cô đúng không?
Kỹ sư: Cô đúng ạ.
Giáo viên: Cô vẫn là giáo viên mà. Cô không nhịn được.

questions.prompt: How long has the engineer been standing?
questions.explanation: 'I have been standing for an hour.'
questions.hint: Look at the engineer's third turn.

questions.explanation: 'I am going to Saigon for a meeting.'
questions.hint: Look at the engineer's fifth turn.

questions.explanation: 'I am going home. Hue. I have lived there for forty years.'
questions.hint: Look at the teacher's third turn.

questions.explanation: 'Reading. I taught children how to read. For thirty years.'
questions.hint: Look at the teacher's fifth turn.

questions.prompt: What does the teacher mean by 'Reading is never the same thing twice'?
options: Each child reading is a different kind of reader
questions.explanation: 'A child who learns "cat" is different from a child who learns "enough".'
questions.hint: Look at the teacher's long answer about cat and enough.

questions.prompt: How many years does the teacher say it takes a child to go from 'cat' to 'enough'?
questions.explanation: 'The road from "cat" to "enough" takes seven years.'
questions.hint: Look at the teacher's answer about the road.

questions.prompt: Does the teacher miss her job?
options: Some days yes, most days no

questions.prompt: What is the teacher reading on the train?
questions.hint: Look at her reply to 'What are you reading?'

questions.prompt: How did the teacher guess that the man was an engineer?
options: From his shoes — the soles are worn at strange places
questions.explanation: 'Your shoes. They are clean, but the soles are worn at strange places.'
questions.hint: Look at the teacher's reply to 'How did you know?'

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the conversation?
options: Warm and curious — strangers becoming briefly acquainted on a long train
questions.explanation: Both speakers are polite; the teacher even guesses his job, and admits, 'I am still a teacher. I cannot help it.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: The teacher says 'reading is never the same thing twice' — a child who knows the word 'cat' is a different reader from one who knows 'enough'. Think of one skill you have grown in over years. What is the difference between the early version of you with that skill, and the version of you now?
